seoTitle: How to Create a Microsoft Loop Workspace (2026)
metaDescription: Set up a new collaborative workspace in Microsoft Loop, from naming the project to auto-attaching relevant files and adding a cover icon.
intro: When a new project needs its own space to organize pages, tables, and tracking, Microsoft Loop workspaces give you a central container to bundle everything together. This guide covers creating a fresh workspace, naming it clearly, and even letting Loop automatically find related files to attach.

steps.heading: Open the Loop dashboard
steps.body: Go to loop.cloud.microsoft and sign in to access your central application directory.

steps.heading: Start a new workspace
steps.body: Click the plus icon in the top right workspace header panel to open the setup drawer.

steps.heading: Name your workspace
steps.body: Type a custom project name and add a header icon or emoji to give the workspace a distinct visual identity.

steps.heading: Let Loop auto-attach relevant files
steps.body: Use the integrated file discovery box to let Loop scan and attach relevant Office documents to your new workspace automatically.

steps.heading: Create and review
steps.body: Click Create to compile your new workspace, then check the sidebar and initial page tree to confirm everything set up correctly.

faq.question: Can I create a workspace without any pages in it yet?
faq.answer: Yes, you can start with a blank workspace and add pages later, or use the smart search feature to auto-populate it with related files.

faq.question: What does the smart file discovery feature actually do?
faq.answer: It scans your account for relevant Office documents and can automatically attach them to your new workspace to save setup time.

faq.question: Can I add team members while creating the workspace?
faq.answer: You can add collaborators as part of setup or invite them afterward once the workspace and initial pages are in place.

faq.question: Is there a limit to how many workspaces I can create?
faq.answer: The description doesn't specify a hard limit, but each workspace acts as its own container, so it's worth keeping your structure organized rather than creating too many overlapping ones.
